guideline_references: JBDS-IP: The Management of the Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS) in Adults (2022)
ADS/Diabetes Australia guidance on hyperglycaemic emergencies
intentional_test_features: Discrimination trap: the insidious multi-day course, absence of Kussmaul breathing and absence of acetone breath must steer the reasoner AWAY from diabetic ketoacidosis toward a hyperosmolar picture — anchoring on 'diabetic with high sugar = DKA' is the error under test.

Red-herring load: global altered mental status can be misread as an acute stroke, and a suspected urinary infection can be misread as the sole cause (urosepsis) rather than the precipitant of a hyperglycaemic emergency.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours clinician at a nursing home who verified capillary glucose and activated a Priority 1 ambulance; the AI Doctor cannot take vitals, examine, or give IV fluids and is scored on recognising a life-threatening hyperglycaemic emergency and escalating (000) while directing carer-deliverable measures only.
Age not stated in source ('elderly') — inferred as older_adult; exact age is a placeholder pending clinician confirmation.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-035.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
